Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis Alaaeldin A.E. Elozeiri a,*

, Seline Zijderveld dos Santos a

, Jouke E. Dykstra a, Huub H.M. Rijnaarts a, Rob G.H. Lammertink b,** a Environmental Technology, Wageningen University & Research, Bornse Weilanden 9, 6708 WG, Wageningen, the Netherlandsb Membrane Science and Technology, Faculty of Science and Technology (TNW), University of Twente, Drienerlolaan 5, 7522 NB, Enschede, the Netherlands

A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords:Polyelectrolyte multilayerCo-ion leakageWater permeanceElectrodialysisDiffusion dialysis

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

content: A B S T R A C T Ion-exchange membranes with high monovalent/divalent selectivity can accelerate the commercialization of electrochemical systems for circular economies. The membrane selectivity can be achieved by coating the membrane with a polyelectrolyte multilayer (PEM). The co-ion and water transport are critical aspects for using the coated cation-exchange membranes (CEMs) in bipolar membrane electrodialysis stacks (BMED) that utilize brine streams containing multivalent cations. In BMED, the CEM is needed to minimize the hydroxide and multivalent cation transport while facilitating the monovalent cation transport. We examined the ion and water transport across bare and coated CEMs in diffusion dialysis and electrodialysis. The CEMs were coated on one side with PAH/PSS multilayers, i.e., poly(allylamine) and poly(4-styrenesulfonate). During diffusion dialysis experiments, the ion flux across bare CEMs depends mainly on the type of anion, where OH-> Cl-> SO42-. Moreover, the ion diffusion coefficients inside a CEM were analyzed using a Nernst-Planck model fitted to the diffusion dialysis and membrane resistance. The cation mobility is less restricted than the anion mobility inside the FKB membrane, where the trend of diffusion coefficients is Na+ ≫ Mg2+, Cl-> SO42-. In PEM-coated membranes, the ion fluxes were sensitive to the coating orientation as well as the cation type. Furthermore, the PEM coating did not affect the osmotic transport of water across the coated CEMs.

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

content: the coating orientation as well as the cation type. Furthermore, the PEM coating did not affect the osmotic transport of water across the coated CEMs. In other words, the water transport is limited by the permeance of the thick CEM rather than the thin PAH/PSS multilayer coating. Lastly, the effect of solution concentration on ion transport across PEM-coated membranes was examined considering the Donnan exclusion mechanism.

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

content: 1. Introduction Ion-exchange membranes (IEMs) are key components for several electrochemical technologies in water and energy applications, e.g., electrodialysis [1–4], membrane electrolysis [5], and flow batteries [6,7]. The membrane is characterized by a high concentration of fixed-charged groups, either cationic or anionic. The function of an ion-exchange membrane is to promote counter-ion transport while restricting the water and co-ion leakages. Out of the common anions, hydroxides transport across cation-exchange membranes is of special interest. Depending on the application, the CEM is either designed to selectively facilitate the OH-transport [8] or to minimize the OH- leakage [9,10]. The next generation of ion-exchange membranes is required to support additional functionalities. An interesting application for a multifunctional membrane is bipolar membrane electrodialysis (BMED). The BMED process produces acids and bases from a brine stream using electricity [11]. Standard BMED utilizes three types of membranes: a bipolar membrane, an anion-exchange membrane, and a cation-exchange membrane (CEM). The CEM separates the base stream from the salt/brine stream. The Mg2+ and Ca2+ ions can ruin the energy and current efficiency in BMED due to their high scaling potential through the formation of Mg(OH)2 and CaCO3 precipitates. Hence, caustic is commonly dosed as pretreatment for the brine stream to precipitate the Mg2+ and Ca2+ ions before they reach the BMED unit [12,13].

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

content: . Hence, caustic is commonly dosed as pretreatment for the brine stream to precipitate the Mg2+ and Ca2+ ions before they reach the BMED unit [12,13]. A CEM that can restrict both the hydroxide transport as well as the Mg2+ and Ca2+ transport is of high interest. Such a membrane will facilitate the direct use of brine in BMED without losing expensive NaOH in the brine pretreatment step.

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

content: Polyelectrolyte multilayer (PEM) modifications can bring additional functionality to ion-exchange membranes: monovalent selective trans­port [14–16] and antifouling characteristics [17–19]. The PEM structure is formed by depositing layers of polycations and polyanions, alter­nately, via the layer-by-layer technique [20,21]. In electrodialysis, the Na+/Ca2+ flux selectivity across a CEM was enhanced from 0.64 (bare CEM) to 1.3 (PEM-coated CEM) [15]. Moreover, the Na+/Mg2+ selec­tivity was improved from 0.5 (bare CEM) to 1.7 (PEM-coated CEM) [21]. The monovalent/divalent selectivity can be further enhanced by tuning the PEM coating conditions as well as by selecting the optimal CEM to be coated with PEM. Coating a CEM that has a low water volume fraction leads to a more loose PEM structure, and consequently, a lower PEM resistance [16]. Under applied electric current, the PEM imposes different resistances to ions depending on the ion charge and hydration shell. Generally, the resistance of PEM-coated membranes increases in the following order of cations: K+ < Na+ ≪ Ca2+ < Mg2+ [15,16,20–22]. In our previous work [16], we analyzed the counter-ion transport across CEMs coated on one side with PAH/PSS multilayers. Interest­ingly, the PEM coating restricts the transport of multivalent cations, e.g., Mg2+, only when the coating faces the Mg2+ transport direction, i.e., the forward transport direction.

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

content: cts the transport of multivalent cations, e.g., Mg2+, only when the coating faces the Mg2+ transport direction, i.e., the forward transport direction. In the forward transport direction, the coated membrane exhibits low limiting current densities, below 4 mA/cm2 in single electrolyte solutions of 0.25 M Mg2+ or Ca2+. To efficiently integrate the PEM-coated CEMs in BMED stacks, the PEM coating should be oriented towards the salt compartment to restrict the Mg2+ and Ca2+ transport. The co-ion (hydroxide) leakage across the CEMs, among other fac­tors, can reduce the current efficiency of the BMED stack [23,24]. Moreover, the water transport across the CEMs and AEMs in BMED stacks is undesired [25–28]. On one hand, when the ion concentrations in the salt stream are much higher than the base stream, water is transported via osmosis from the base to the salt stream, which dilutes the brine stream, and resists the transport of counter-ions (cations) as the counter-ion transport via migration is opposed by the convection forces. Consequently, the water transport results in a higher energy consumption in BMED stacks. On the other hand, the water transport in the opposite direction, i.e., from the salt to the base stream, dilutes the product stream (base). In this study, we investigate the water and co-ion transport across PEM-coated cation-exchange membranes with a focus on the coating orientation.

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

content: se). In this study, we investigate the water and co-ion transport across PEM-coated cation-exchange membranes with a focus on the coating orientation. Furthermore, we explain the interplay between the CEM properties, e.g., ionic charge density, and performance parameters, e.g., the water and hydroxide permeances. This study guides the membrane selection and design for alkali-proof membranes in BMED and alkaliselective membranes in base recovery applications. 2. Materials & methods The investigated membranes are: Fumasep FKB-PK-130, FKS-PET130, and FKD-PK-75 (Fumatech BWT GmbH, Germany), Selemion CMTE and CMVN (Asahi Glass Co., Japan), and Fujifilm CEM type-10 and type12 (Fujifilm Manufacturing Europe BV, The Netherlands). The mem­brane properties, e.g., membrane thickness, ionic charge density, and resistance, were characterized in our previous work [29]. Solutions were prepared using Milli-Q water (Millipore) and the chemical of interest. The following reagent grade chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich: Na2SO4, MgCl2, NaOH, poly(allylamine hydrochloride) (PAH, ~50 kDa), and poly(4-styrenesulfonic acid) solution (PSS, ~75 kDa, 18 wt% in H2O, 1.11 g/mL at 25 ◦C). Sodium chloride was pur­chased from VWR Chemicals. 2.1. Layer-by-layer coating We coated one side of different commercial cation-exchange mem­branes (CEMs) with poly(allylamine) (PAH)/poly (4-styrenesulfonate) (PSS) polyelectrolyte multilayer, as detailed in our previous study [16].

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

content: Each membrane was held horizontally using rubber gaskets and frames made of acrylonitrile butadiene styrene material (Ultimaker ABS). Firstly, a polycation layer was deposited on the cation-exchange mem­brane by pouring a solution of 1.9 g/L PAH (0.02 M PAH monomer unit) in 1 M NaCl, pH 2.3, on the top membrane surface. For each coating step, the polyelectrolyte solutions were left on the membrane for 5 min. After each coating step, the membrane surface was rinsed thoroughly with Milli-Q for 1 min to remove weakly attached polyelectrolytes. After­ward, a polyanion layer was deposited using a solution of 4.1 g/L PSS (0.02 M PSS monomer unit) in 0.5 M NaCl, pH 2.3. A total of 6.5 bilayers (i.e., 13 layers) were deposited on one side of the CEMs.

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

content: 2.2. Diffusion experiments Diffusion experiments were conducted to characterize the ion and water permeability across different membranes. Bare and PEM-coated samples of FKB membrane were investigated in single electrolyte solu­tions of NaCl, MgCl2, Na2SO4, or NaOH. As the high solution pH can destabilize the PEM coating [30], the membrane samples used in NaOH experiments were not reused in other experiments afterward. We examined the influence of the coating orientations with respect to the ion transport direction. Furthermore, we analyzed the NaOH and water permeability across different bare CEMs: CMVN, CMTE, FKB, Fuji-CEM-10, and Fuji-CEM-12. The dialysis experiments were per­formed in triplicate. Before the diffusion experiment, the membrane samples were equilibrated in the concentrated solution of interest: 0.5 M NaCl, 0.25 M MgCl2, 0.25 M Na2SO4, and 0.5 M NaOH. To avoid having excess salt inside the membranes, the membrane samples were then submerged in the corresponding 1000x diluted solution, e.g., 0.5 mM NaCl. The membranes were then assembled into an H-cell configuration (Fig. 1a) where the membrane separates two compartments: the concentrate (e. g., 0.5 M NaCl) and the diluate (e.g., 0.5 mM NaCl). A rubber ring was placed between the membrane and the side neck of the bottle to limit water losses. The active membrane area was 8.55 cm2. Known masses of the solution of interest (c.a., 130 g) were poured into the respective compartments. Each compartment was capped.

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

content: e area was 8.55 cm2. Known masses of the solution of interest (c.a., 130 g) were poured into the respective compartments. Each compartment was capped. Moreover, a Teflon tape was wrapped around the cell joints to minimize water losses. The ex­periments were conducted at ambient lab temperature, i.e., 19–22 ◦C, for 2–3 days. In both compartments, the solutions were stirred at 150 rpm to reduce the diffusion boundary layer thickness next to the mem­brane surface. Over the experiment duration, samples of known volume were withdrawn from the diluate in order to analyze the ion concen­tration. Except for the NaOH experiments, the anion concentrations in the diluate were analyzed using ion chromatography (Dionex). For NaOH experiments, the Na+ concentration in the diluate was analyzed using inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectrometry (Per­kinElmer ICP-OES). At the end of the experiment, the solution mass in each compartment was measured gravimetrically. The average ion and water flux over the experiment are calculated as detailed in the sup­porting information, S1. The measured water flux in the diffusion dial­ysis experiments, Jw,net [m3/(m2.s)], is a combination of the osmotic and electro-osmotic water fluxes Jw,net = Jw,o + Jw,eEq. 1 Under osmosis, the water moves from the diluate to the concentrate.

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

content: bination of the osmotic and electro-osmotic water fluxes Jw,net = Jw,o + Jw,eEq. 1 Under osmosis, the water moves from the diluate to the concentrate. The osmotic water flux, Jw,o [m3/(m2.s)], is driven by the osmotic pressure difference generated by the ion concentration difference be­tween the diluate and concentrate. The relation between the osmotic water flux and the osmotic pressure difference can be simplified with the water permeance, Aw [m/(s.Pa)], as a proportionality constant [31–33]. Jw,o = Aw⋅ΔΠEq. 2 The osmotic pressure difference between the concentrate and the diluate, ΔΠ [Pa], is evaluated as follows [34,35]

In addition to osmosis, water molecules move via electro-osmosis while being associated with ion transport either as part of the ion hy­dration shell or being dragged by the ion [36,37]. The electro-osmotic flux across IEMs is quantified as follows [38] Jw,e = Vw⋅∑ i Ji⋅nw,iEq. 4 where Ji [mol/(m2.s)] is the ion flux, nw,i [mol H2O/mol ion] is the hydration number of ion (i), Vw [m3/mol H2O] is the molar volume of water, i.e., 18 mL/mol H2O [38]. The electro-osmotic flux is in the same direction as the ion flux, i.e., from the concentrate to the diluate. We set the hydration numbers of Na+, Mg2+, Cl-, and SO42-to 6, 15.5, 8, and 13 mol H2O/mol ion, respectively, based on the study of Han et al. [38], where the hydration numbers were fitted to the experimental water flux during electrodialysis. Assuming a proportionality between the ion hy­dration number and the Stokes radius, the OH-hydration number is estimated as follows nw,OH-= nw,Cl-⋅ΓStOH-ΓStCl-Eq. 5 The Stokes radius of the ion, ΓSti [m], is calculated as follows [39,40] ΓSti =κB⋅T6π⋅μ0⋅Di

2.3. Cyclic voltammetry and chronopotentiometry We investigated the effect of solution concentration on the currentvoltage response of different coated membranes using a 6-compartment electrodialysis cell (6C-ED, further details are provided in Refs. [16,29]). We used cyclic voltammetry to analyze the current-voltage response of coated and bare membranes in the single electrolyte solutions of NaCl, Na2SO4, or MgCl2, where the cation concentration was either 0.2 or 0.5

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

content: M equivalent. Furthermore, the transient membrane response was investigated via chronopotentiometry. The steady-state current-voltage values based on chronopotentiometry are compared to the cyclic vol­tammograms in the Supporting Information, S3. Before each measurement, the membrane samples were equilibrated in the solution of interest overnight. Afterward, the membrane was assembled between the middle compartments of the 6C-ED cell. The active membrane area was 23.2 ± 0.1 cm2 without an area reducer, and 4.52 ± 0.04 cm2 with an area reducer. The solutions were recirculated at 270 ± 10 mL/min. The solution temperature was kept at 20 ± 0.5 ◦C using heat exchangers. The cyclic voltammetry curves were scanned with a step of 10 mV at a rate of 5 or 0.5 mV/s using a potentiostat (Ivium Technologies, The Netherlands). The effect of solution flow rate was investigated for FKB-6.5 BL in 0.1 M MgCl2 by measuring the membrane response at solution flow rates of 150, 270, and 390 mL/min. 3. Results & discussion 3.1. Transport in diffusion dialysis In this section, we analyze the transport parameters for cations and anions across bare and coated cation-exchange membranes using a diffusion dialysis setup (Fig. 1a). At the start of the diffusion experiment, the cation concentration in the concentrate was 0.5 M equivalent, while the diluate was 1000x less. Ions diffused towards the diluate following their concentration gradient, while the water flowed towards the concentrate.

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

content: the diluate was 1000x less. Ions diffused towards the diluate following their concentration gradient, while the water flowed towards the concentrate. We tested the diffusion of four different single electrolytes (Fig. 1b). For the same dialysis duration and starting concentration difference, the average OH-flux was 3.7x higher than the Cl-flux (Fig. 1b), which can be explained by the difference in ion mobilities and hydration numbers. In aqueous solutions, the OH-exhibits 2.6x higher mobility than the Cl-[41]. The ion mobility inside the membrane is reduced by a factor compared to its mobility in bulk solution, depending on the ion hydration radius and membrane water volume fraction [42]. As chlorides have larger hydrated radii compared to hydroxides [43], the chloride mobility inside the membrane is further reduced compared to the hydroxide mobility. Common ions, e.g., Cl-and Na+, move in the solution with a hydration shell, which limits the ion mobility [34]. Instead, protons can move via the hopping/Grotthuss shuttling mecha­nism, where the proton is transported from a hydronium ion to a neighboring water molecule [44]. Traditionally, it was assumed that hydroxides move via hopping, similar to protons, where the hydroxide accepts a proton from one of the neighboring water molecules [34]. Recently, studies debated the transport mechanisms of hydroxides and the significance of the hopping versus the Brownian/vehicular

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

content: Fig. 1. (a) An illustration of the diffusion experiment. The ions diffuse from the concentrate to diluate while the net water transport is in the opposite direction. Average (b) anion and (c) water fluxes over the FKB membrane during a single electrolyte experiment of NaOH, NaCl, MgCl2, or Na2SO4. The diffusion experiments were performed for three cases: bare cation-exchange membranes (circles), as well as one-side coated membranes with the coating facing either the concentrate (squares) or the diluate (triangles). The coating is composed of 6.5 PAH/PSS bilayers. The negative values of the water fluxes reflect the opposite direction of the water transport with respect to the direction of ion transport. A.A.E. Elozeiri et al. Journal of Membrane Science 741 (2026) 125072 mechanism [45–50]. Hydroxides have lower mobility than protons, as the vehicular transport mechanism is believed to be more significant for hydroxide compared to proton transport. We coated the CEM on one side with a PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer (PEM). The PAH/PSS multilayer is unstable at high pH [30], where the PAH loses its ionization [51]. In this regard, the PEM coating was expected to mildly reduce or not affect the NaOH diffusion as the membrane will be exposed to high pHs in the NaOH experiments. Based on the dialysis experiment, the NaOH flux was 1.2 times higher in case of the PEM-coated membrane compared to the uncoated membrane whether the coating is facing the concentrate or the diluate.

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

content: as 1.2 times higher in case of the PEM-coated membrane compared to the uncoated membrane whether the coating is facing the concentrate or the diluate. Therefore, the membrane was more permeable for the NaOH transport after it was coated with the PAH/PSS multilayers. The increase in the membrane permeability after the coating can be a result of exposing the membrane to a low pH during the coating procedure (pH 2.3), followed by a high pH during the NaOH dialysis experiment (pH 10.7–13.7). For bare-membrane experiments, the average anion flux in case of NaCl was 1.4x higher than that of MgCl2, while it was 61x higher than that of Na2SO4 (Fig. 1b). Evidently, the salt diffusion across ionexchange membranes is more limited by the co-ion type than the type of counter-ion based on their corresponding concentration inside the membrane. For NaCl and MgCl2 experiments, the PEM coating reduced the salt flux compared to the uncoated membranes since the PEM coating imposes additional resistance against the ion transport (Fig. 1b). Moreover, the coating orientation led to different effects for each salt. The PAH is a weak polycation, while the PSS is a strong polyanion [51]. The presence of excess polycationic or polyanionic charges in the PEM can be compensated with mobile ions, i.e., extrinsic compensation [52]. The applied ionic strength and pH in coating solutions develop a PEM structure with an effective positive charge [16].

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

content: ., extrinsic compensation [52]. The applied ionic strength and pH in coating solutions develop a PEM structure with an effective positive charge [16]. The bottleneck for NaCl diffusion across the PEM-coated membrane is the Cl-diffusion across the CEM. The net positive charge of the PEM layer increased the Cl- concentration at the PEM/CEM interface, which resulted in higher NaCl flux when the PEM is facing the concentrate relative to the opposite orientation. Compared to NaCl, the coating orientation affected the MgCl2 transport differently (Fig. 1b). There are two limitations on the MgCl2 transport across the PEM-coated membranes in the diffusion dialysis. Firstly, the Cl-transport is limited across the CEM due to the low co-ion concentration, i.e., Cl-, inside the CEM. Secondly, the Mg2+ transport is limited by the PEM coating, especially when the PEM is facing the transport direction of Mg2+ ions, as it leads to a lower Mg2+ concen­tration at the PEM/CEM interface. The PEM imposes a relatively high resistance against Mg2+ mobility compared to that of Na+ because Mg2+ ions have a larger hydration shell [16]. Moreover, the Mg2+ concen­tration in the PEM coating is lower than that of Na+ based on the positive charge of the PEM and the Donnan exclusion mechanism. In the Na2SO4 dialysis experiment, the influence of the PEM coating was negligible as the SO42-transport was highly restricted by the cation-exchange membrane.

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

content: 2SO4 dialysis experiment, the influence of the PEM coating was negligible as the SO42-transport was highly restricted by the cation-exchange membrane. In the diffusion dialysis experiments, the net water flow was in the opposite direction with respect to the ion fluxes (Fig. 1c). The PEM coating influence on the water transport was insignificant. In other words, the water transport is limited by the dense cation-exchange membrane, while the PEM coating does not add a significant resis­tance to water transport. This is different from the PEM-based nano­filtration membranes, where ultrafiltration membranes are coated with a PEM until the base membrane pores are fully covered [53]. Unlike PEM-coated CEMs, the water transport across PEM-coated ultrafiltration membranes is mainly limited by the PEM domain [54,55]. The osmotic water flux across a membrane is proportional to the water permeance (Aw) and the osmotic pressure difference. We analyzed the water permeance of the membrane at each electrolyte by solving Eq. (1) – Eq. (4). The membrane water permeance varied in range of [2.2–3.5]*10-9 m/(s.bar) in case of Na+ as counter-ion, while it was [1.2–1.5]*10-9 m/(s.bar) in case of Mg2+ (Fig. 2c). Compared to Na+, Mg2+ ions exhibit a larger hydration shell, which can reduce the free water fraction inside the membrane [29,38].

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

content: of Mg2+ (Fig. 2c). Compared to Na+, Mg2+ ions exhibit a larger hydration shell, which can reduce the free water fraction inside the membrane [29,38]. Moreover, the low mobility of Mg2+ inside the membrane is hypothesized to retard the water transport across the membrane, similar to the effect of Mg2+ on the surrounding faster cations inside CEMs [42]. In our previous work [42], we calculated counter-ion diffusion co­efficients inside different cation-exchange membranes based on direct current resistance measurements. We assumed an ideal Donnan equi­librium condition where all ion activity coefficients were set to 1. In the present study, we compile the resistance measurement (performed in Ref. [42]) with the diffusion dialysis data (performed in this study) to further analyze the co-ion transport at NaCl, MgCl2, and Na2SO4 elec­trolytes. In this regard, we evaluate the ion diffusion coefficients and activity coefficients inside the membrane by jointly fitting two sets of experiments: the diffusion dialysis and resistance measurements. The average ion fluxes of the diffusion experiment, Ji [mol/(m2.s)], are modeled via the linearized Nernst-Planck equation [34] as follows Ji = -Dmi ⋅ΔCmiδm -ziFCm,avgiDmiRT⋅Δφm δm

The fitting approach is detailed in the supporting information, S2. In Fig. 2a, the measured concentrations from the diffusion dialysis experiment are compared to 1D transport model results, where we

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

content: Fig. 2. (a) Anion concentration in the diluate during the diffusion dialysis experiments with a single electrolyte of NaCl, MgCl2, or Na2SO4. The starting anion concentration was 0.5 mM and 0.5 M equivalent in the diluate and concentrate, respectively. The diluate and concentrate were separated by a standard cationexchange membrane, i.e., FKB. The markers represent the experimental results, while the dashed lines refer to the model results. (b) Fitting values for the ion diffusion coefficients inside FKB membranes. (c) Water permeance of an FKB membrane in a single electrolyte of NaCl, MgCl2, or Na2SO4. A.A.E. Elozeiri et al. Journal of Membrane Science 741 (2026) 125072 utilized the fitted diffusion and activity coefficients as model inputs. The model is described in our previous work [56]. The fitted activity co­efficients and diffusion coefficients inside FKB membrane are reported in Table 1 and Fig. 2b. The Na+ diffusion coefficient in the membrane is an order of magnitude higher than that of Mg2+. Compared to the ion mobility in aqueous solution, the Na+ inside the membrane is reduced by a factor of 11, while the Mg2+ mobility is reduced by a factor of 43 (Table S2). The ion mobility reduction in the membrane is caused by the membrane tortuosity [29] as well as the ion hydration volume and en­ergy [42]. The co-ion mobility is reduced by a factor of c.a. 130 for Cl- and SO42-. As a counter-ion leaves its position, another counter-ion moves to replace it [42,57].

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

content: mobility is reduced by a factor of c.a. 130 for Cl- and SO42-. As a counter-ion leaves its position, another counter-ion moves to replace it [42,57]. As the co-ion concentration is much lower than that of counter-ions, co-ions are less likely to exchange positions with a surrounding ion. Instead, the co-ion transport is expected to incur more friction with the membrane polymer. The ion activity coefficients inside the membrane can be used to calculate the ion concentrations via the Donnan equilibrium condition (Eq. S10). At ideal conditions (at infinite dilution), the ion activity co­efficients equal 1. Our fitting resulted in a co-ion activity coefficient of 1.8 for the FKB membrane in MgCl2 solution (Table 1). A higher co-ion activity coefficient inside the membrane translates to a lower co-ion concentration inside the membrane. Therefore, the co-ion concentra­tion in the membrane based on an activity coefficient of 1.8 is lower than the predictions assuming ideality (activity coefficient of 1). Possibly, the low co-ion concentration (relative to the ideal predictions) is a result of the limited space inside the cation-exchange membrane of FKB, espe­cially in the presence of counter-ions of a relatively large hydration shell, such as Mg2+ ions. 3.2.

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

content: inside the cation-exchange membrane of FKB, espe­cially in the presence of counter-ions of a relatively large hydration shell, such as Mg2+ ions. 3.2. Influence of ion concentration on PEM resistance The PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer coatings impose high resistance towards the transport of multivalent cations, e.g., Mg2+ and Ca2+, with a negligible increase in the resistance towards monovalent cations, e.g., Na+ and K+ [16]. In a 0.1 M Mg2+ solution, the current-voltage response of the PEM-coated CEM exhibited an asym­metric behavior with respect to the current direction (Fig. 3a). When the PEM coating is facing the Mg2+ transport direction (forward transport, Fig. S2b), the current-voltage curve reaches a limiting current density as the Mg2+ ions are depleted in the PEM at the PEM/CEM interface. Increasing the solution flow rate reduces the diffusion boundary layer at the membrane surface. However, increasing the solution flow rate did not influence the limiting current density of the PEM-coated CEM in 0.1 M MgCl2 (Fig. 3a). Within the current range of ±4 mA/cm2, the current-voltage response of the bare FKB membranes is linear in 0.1–0.25 M MgCl2 solutions (Fig. 3b). Hence, the bare membranes were operated in the under-limiting current regime, and there are sufficient ions in the diffusion boundary layers to conduct the current.

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

content: e membranes were operated in the under-limiting current regime, and there are sufficient ions in the diffusion boundary layers to conduct the current. The limiting current behavior of the PEM-coated membranes in MgCl2 solutions is a result of the ion depletion inside the PEM coating, and it does not depend on the diffusion boundary layers. The coated membrane response can be described by two parameters: the area resistance and the limiting current density. In MgCl2 solution, the limiting current density of the PEM-coated membrane increases as the solution concentration increases (Fig. 3b). For FKB coated with 6.5 PAH/PSS bilayers, increasing the Mg2+ concentration in solution by

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

content: 2.5x (from 0.1 to 0.25 M) leads to a 2.3x increase in limiting current density (from 0.25 to 0.58 mA/cm2, Fig. S5a). In bipolar membrane electrodialysis (BMED), high current densities, in the range of 20–100 mA/cm2, are typically applied to operate the bipolar membrane in the over-limiting current regime [12,58,59]. To evaluate the potential application of the PEM-coated membranes in BMED stacks, we scanned the cyclic voltammograms of the bare and coated membranes in 0.2 and 0.5 M NaCl solutions in a wide range of currents, i.e., ±40 and ±80 mA/cm2, respectively (Fig. 3c). In NaCl solutions, the current-voltage response of the coated membrane is similar to that of the bare membranes. Unlike the transport of multi­valent cations, the PAH/PSS coatings do not impose significant resis­tance towards Na+ transport. The current-voltage curves of the coated and uncoated FKB membranes exhibit a limiting current regime at high applied current densities due to the ion depletion in the diffusion boundary layer. In a spacer-free channel with a linear solution velocity of c.a. 0.4 cm/s, the limiting current density of the bare FKB membrane was 25 ± 1 mA/cm2 in 0.2 M NaCl (Fig. S5b). The applied current density in BMED should be high enough to operate the bipolar mem­brane in the overlimiting current regime.

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

content: 0.2 M NaCl (Fig. S5b). The applied current density in BMED should be high enough to operate the bipolar mem­brane in the overlimiting current regime. At the same time, applied current density should be below the limiting current density of the PEM-coated CEMs, which depends on the ion concentrations in the feed stream of the BMED stack, the solution flow rate, and the spacer hy­drodynamics [60–62]. The total area resistance of the coated membrane, Λm+PEM [Ω.m2], is calculated as follows [16] Λm+PEM = Am⋅(rm+PEM+s -rs)Eq. 8 where Am [m2] is the active membrane area, rm+PEM+s [Ω] is the com­bined resistance of the coated membrane and solution, and rs [Ω] is the resistance measured in the stack with the solution but without the membrane in place. In the present study, the membrane resistances are calculated based on direct current measurements, which account for the membrane resistance as well as the interfacial resistances at the mem­brane surface, i.e., the electrical double layer and the diffusion boundary layer [29,63–65]. In MgCl2 solutions, the combined resistance, rm+PEM+s, is evaluated based on the slope of the current-voltage data points in the reverse direction, i.e., [0, -4 mA/cm2], as explained in Ref. [16]. In NaCl solutions, the combined resistance is evaluated based on the ohmic range of currents, i.e., [-10, 10 mA/cm2], in order to reduce the effect of the ion depletion in the diffusion boundary layer on the membrane resistance calculations.

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

content: s, i.e., [-10, 10 mA/cm2], in order to reduce the effect of the ion depletion in the diffusion boundary layer on the membrane resistance calculations. In MgCl2 solutions, increasing the solution concentration reduces the coated membrane resistance, especially for low resistance membranes, e.g., FKD (Fig. 3d). The influence of the solution concentration on the LCD and membrane area resistance of the coated membranes is a consequence of the ion partitioning between the solution and the PEM domain. The PEM domain has a net positive charge, which limits the presence of the cations according to the Donnan exclusion mechanism. The multivalent cations are further excluded compared to the mono­valent cations. Furthermore, the ions with larger hydration radius and energy, e.g., Mg2+, have lower mobility compared to ions with smaller hydration shells, e.g., Na+. The coated membrane resistance increases by about an order of magnitude when the cation type is changed from Na+ to Mg2+ (Fig. 3e). The PAH/PSS coating is selective for the transport of the monovalent cations, e.g., Na+, over the multivalent ones, e.g., Mg2+, based on the Donnan exclusion mechanism and the difference in the ion mobilities. Regarding the influence of the anion type, the coated membrane resis­tance did not change significantly in response to changing the anion type from Cl-to SO42-.

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

content: the influence of the anion type, the coated membrane resis­tance did not change significantly in response to changing the anion type from Cl-to SO42-. Moreover, the current-voltage response of the coated membrane was linear and symmetric with respect to the current direc­tion in 0.5 M Na+, whether the anion is Cl-or SO42-(Fig. 3c, Fig. S6). Therefore, the anion charge, i.e., -1 or -2, does not affect the cation transport across PEM-coated CEMs in electrodialysis application. That is

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

content: NaCl1.01.0MgCl21.01.8Na2SO41.01.4 A.A.E. Elozeiri et al. Journal of Membrane Science 741 (2026) 125072 totally different from the diffusion dialysis applications, where the cation and anion fluxes across the CEMs strongly depend on the anion charge based on the Donnan exclusion mechanism (Fig. 1b). 3.3. Hydroxide leakage in CEMs The co-ion leakage in CEMs is a critical aspect for hydroxide-related applications. We characterized the NaOH and water diffusion across 6 different commercial CEMs. The NaOH flux depends on the partitioning inside the membrane as well as the NaOH permeance, BNaOH [m/s], as follows JNaOH = BNaOH⋅ΔCmNaOHEq. 9 where JNaOH [mol/m2.s] is the average NaOH flux across the CEM, and ΔCmNaOH [mol/m3] is the NaOH concentration difference between the two membrane sides. The co-ion partitioning mainly depends on the membrane ionic charge density, Cmfix. For the NaOH experiment, the OH- concentrations inside the membrane are evaluated via the ideal Donnan equilibrium and the electroneutrality condition as follows [29] CmNa+ ⋅CmOH-= CNa+⋅COH-Eq. 10 CmNa+ -CmOH--Cmfix = 0Eq. 11 The membrane resistance, as well as the NaOH and water per­meances, are connected to the membrane water volume fraction via the membrane tortuosity and the membrane-ion or membrane-water fric­tion [42]. The membrane resistance in NaCl is a commonly measured parameter for CEMs.

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

content: membrane tortuosity and the membrane-ion or membrane-water fric­tion [42]. The membrane resistance in NaCl is a commonly measured parameter for CEMs. In this regard, correlating the membrane NaOH permeance to the membrane resistance in NaCl is useful to benchmark the commercial or novel CEMs. Based on characterized membranes in this study, the NaOH and water permeances are highly correlated to the membrane resistance via power law fittings (Fig. 4a) Aw = 8.0159⋅Λ-1.276m,NaCl, with R2D = 0.96Eq. 12 BNaOH = 6.6831⋅Λ-1.074m,NaCl, with R2D = 0.94Eq. 13 where Aw [1E-9 m/(s.bar)] is the water permeance of the membrane in NaOH electrolyte, Λm,NaCl [Ω.cm2] is the membrane area resistance in 0.5 M NaCl as measured via the direct current method, BNaOH [1E-6 m/s] is the NaOH permeance, and R2D is the coefficient of determination. The permeance is an extensive property of the membrane that depends on the membrane thickness. The permeability is an intensive property of the material, as it is normalized for the membrane thickness as follows βw = Aw⋅δmEq. 14 βNaOH = BNaOH⋅δmEq. 15 where βw and βNaOH are the water and NaOH permeabilities, respec­tively. The water and NaOH permeabilities are linearly correlated to the

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

content: Na+ diffusion coefficient as follows (Fig. S7) βw = 0.0416⋅DmNa+ -0.3711, with R2D = 0.94Eq. 16 βNaOH = 0.035⋅DmNa+ + 0.1314, with R2D = 1.00Eq. 17 where DmNa+ [1E-12 m2/s] is the Na+ diffusion coefficient inside the membrane, βw [1E-13 m2/(s.bar)] and βNaOH [1E-10 m2/s] are the water and NaOH permeabilities across a CEM, respectively. The Na+ diffusion coefficients inside the membrane are calculated based on the measured membrane resistance (direct current method) at 0.5 M NaCl according to Ref. [42]. The water content, ionic charge density (ICD), and thickness of an IEM can be tuned to achieve a specific co-ion flux based on the application requirements. The higher the membrane ICD is, the lower the co-ion partitioning inside the membrane becomes (Eq. [ФОРМУЛА: \begin{equation}\frac{\partial C_i}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial J_i}{\partial x} + R_i, \qquad i=1,\dots,4\tag{10}\end{equation}], Eq. [ФОРМУЛА: \begin{equation}J_i = -D_i \frac{\partial C_i}{\partial x} - \frac{z_i F D_i}{RT} C_i \frac{\partial \varphi}{\partial x}, \qquad i=1,\dots,4\tag{11}\end{equation}]). Moreover, the lower the membrane water content is, the lower the ion diffusion coefficients and the NaOH permeability are (Fig. S7). Hence, a membrane with a high ICD and a low water volume fraction is ideal to minimize co-ion leakage (Fig. 4b). However, there is a trade-off between the membrane swelling and ICD. Typically, membranes of higher ionic charge densities swell more [66–69].

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

content: g. 4b). However, there is a trade-off between the membrane swelling and ICD. Typically, membranes of higher ionic charge densities swell more [66–69]. In principle, the membrane thickness can be tuned to match the required permeance. Practically, the choice of membrane thickness is limited by further criteria, e.g., the mechanical stability and the aging behavior of the membrane [70,71]. 3.4. Implications for BMED Bipolar membrane electrodialysis (BMED) processes utilize elec­tricity and a pretreated brine stream to produce acid and base [11,72–74]. To maintain a high current efficiency, the BMED stack includes a CEM to retain the produced OH-ions and reduce water crossover. An extensive pretreatment of the brine is currently required to remove the Mg2+ and Ca2+ ions, as they limit the BMED efficiency by forming scales when combined with hydroxides and carbonates, respectively [13,75]. This pretreatment is costly as it consumes a substantial amount of NaOH. To boost the economics and sustainability of this BMED application, the next generation of CEM should include additional functionality by pre­venting the transport of multivalent ions from the brine stream toward the base stream. This functionality can enable the direct usage of un­treated brine in the BMED stack and minimize the cost of brine pre­treatment. In this regard, coating a polyelectrolyte multilayer (PEM) on one side of the CEM is of high interest.

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

content: k and minimize the cost of brine pre­treatment. In this regard, coating a polyelectrolyte multilayer (PEM) on one side of the CEM is of high interest. The CEM hinders the OH- transport from one side, while the PEM coating hinders the Mg2+ and Ca2+ transport from the other side. Fuji-CEM-12 had the lowest NaOH permeance (9E-7 m/s) among the investigated membranes (Fig. 4a). In terms of the isotropic properties of the membrane, the CMVN material has a relatively low OH-permeability and high ICD (Fig. 4b), which is ideal for OH-blocking. The second requirement on the CEMs is to hinder the transport of multivalent cations, e.g., Mg2+, which can be achieved by coating the CEM with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer (Fig. 3d). The choice of the CEM that will be coated with PEM depends on the ICD and water volume fraction of the bare membrane [16]. A high resistance towards Mg2+ transport can be achieved by coating a CEM that has a low water volume fraction and a moderate ICD. In this regard, FKS and Fuji-CEM-12 are good candidates to be coated with the PEM to block the transport of Mg2+ ions. For future work, Fuji-CEM-12 coated with 6.5 PAH/PSS bilayers is expected to provide a good balance to block the OH-and Mg2+ transport in the BMED application. 4. Conclusion We characterized the ion and water transport across coated and bare cation-exchange membranes (CEMs).

citation.title: Water and co-ion transport across ion-exchange membranes coated with PAH/PSS polyelectrolyte multilayer in electrodialysis and diffusion dialysis

content: transport in the BMED application. 4. Conclusion We characterized the ion and water transport across coated and bare cation-exchange membranes (CEMs). The diffusion of salts across bare CEMs is mainly determined by the anion type, e.g., Cl-, SO42-, or OH-, as well as the membrane properties, including the membrane water con­tent and ionic charge density. The ionic charge density determines the anion partitioning inside the CEM. Furthermore, the co-ion permeability across the membrane increases as the membrane water volume fraction increases. We coated different commercial cation-exchange membranes on one side with a polyelectrolyte multilayer (PEM). For uncoated CEMs, the water permeance correlates well with the ionic resistance of the membrane, as both parameters depend on the membrane water content. Coating the CEM with PAH/PSS multilayer did not affect the osmotic water transport. In this regard, the coated PEM structure is hypothesized to have a higher water permeance compared to the CEM domain. CEMs of low water content are proposed to be coated on one side with PAH/PSS multilayers and used in bipolar membrane electro­dialysis (BMED) applications to minimize the transport of the hydrox­ides and problematic multivalent cations, such as Mg2+.
